finding: A categorization based on knowledge and attitudes reveals three distinct perspective groups, where the largest group prefers religion to science, a second equally large group prefers science, and a smaller group endorses both but roots resolution of competing frameworks in religion. These perspectives shape attitudes on politically divisive issues independently of other ideological measures.

A judgment of candidate quality is influenced both by the policy distance between the evaluator and the target and by proximity to the ideological center. The effect of policy distance is asymmetric: opposite-side targets are judged lower and distance is weighted more heavily against them, even after correcting for measurement artifacts.

In emerging democracies with uneven subnational regimes, electoral accountability driven by incumbent performance is weaker in dominant-party-dominated subnational contexts than in competitive ones, because clientelistic linkages displace performance-based voting in the weakly competitive regions.
Exposure to a high physical vantage point increases risk-taking behavior, mediated by an illusory sense of control; this effect is attenuated by abstract mental framing of visual information.

Advertisements showing intermediate process steps (not just before/after endpoints) increase persuasion through fostered process simulation and heightened credibility. However, when speed of outcome is prioritized and achievable, process focus backfires, reducing credibility and persuasion.

A numerical bias in price perception (left-digit effect) is stronger when prices are perceived side-by-side than when retrieved from memory, because stimulus-based judgments rely on perceptual representations whereas memory-based judgments engage rounding via conceptual representations.

Cognitive elaboration on social-media messages from personal sources (friends/family) mediates the effect of source type on willingness to engage in online collective action. High credibility of personal sources strengthens this indirect effect.

Individuals perceive cognitive and motivational biases as more prevalent in others than in themselves, and this asymmetry persists even when they are explicitly informed of the bias mechanism affecting their own judgments.

Perceived realistic threat mediates the relationship between endorsement of positive stereotypes and negative attitudes/emotions toward a target group. Threat transforms positive stereotype attributes into a basis for negative affect.

Statements written in concrete language are judged as more likely true than identical statements in abstract language, and this effect is stronger when a concrete mindset is primed or when spatial proximity is heightened.

Post-hoc retrospective reports of intentional influence on priming tasks correlate with larger effect sizes, but experimental manipulation of actual intent produces different patterns, suggesting participants confabulate explanations rather than accurately report their intentions.

Cultural models of interdependence versus independence predict the relationship between perceived parental pressure and motivation: in interdependent contexts, parental pressure conveys closeness and boosts post-failure motivation, whereas in independent contexts the same pressure carries different relational meaning.

High-level construal promotes acceptance of negative diagnostic feedback when the targeted domain is perceived as changeable, whereas low-level construal promotes dismissal regardless of changeability. Construal level jointly with changeability perception determines openness to self-improvement information.

Genetic versus environmental explanations of criminal behavior have countervailing effects: genetic explanations reduce perceived control but increase internal attribution and reoffense expectation. These opposing causal inferences predict sentencing in opposite directions, yielding no overall sentencing difference.

Beliefs about how to match motivation type to task type generalize across cultures; both East and West show promotion bias. A cultural difference emerges in whether task-motivation fit is recognized for both independent and interdependent outcome frames.

Anchoring effects on estimates are present even when the anchor is not the starting point but merely a reference for comparison (judge higher/lower). The method quantifies anchoring size and reveals a relationship between anchoring magnitude and confidence.

Different grammatical forms of generics in language influence the gender composition of exemplars retrieved from memory, with masculine generics biasing retrieval toward male exemplars more than other forms.

Third-language learners selectively transfer syntax from previously acquired languages based on typological similarity to the target language, rather than from the most recent language or according to cumulative models. Typological proximity drives transfer selectivity.

Blood glucose levels measured in the lab regulate discounting of future rewards: caloric intake (sugar) reduces the present bias whereas non-caloric sweetener increases it. Body-energy budget dynamically shapes temporal preference.

Activating superstitious concepts or practices improves performance on subsequent cognitive and motor tasks. Self-efficacy is the mediating mechanism: superstitions boost confidence, which increases persistence and task performance.
